_work.title: The Esoteric Cosmologies of Asia
_work.creator: various
_work.rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: A cabinet of the great cosmological maps of South and Southeast Asia: the Jain Loka Purusha (the universe imagined as a cosmic giant) and its Adhai-dvipa and Jambudvipa world-charts, the siddhachakra yantra and the Samavasarana; the Buddhist Mount Meru / Sumeru universe in its Tibetan, Burmese and Thai Traibhumi forms, with a Tibetan medical-cosmological thangka; and the Hindu cosmos — the Brahmanda cosmic egg, Vishnu's Vishvarupa universal form, the churning of the milk-ocean, Mount Kailash, and Nataraja's cosmic dance.

entries.title: Loka Purusha (the Cosmic Man / Universe as a Giant)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Jain, India)
entries.summary:
The *Loka Purusha*, the Jain image of the entire universe shaped as a colossal standing man (or, in some accounts, a figure standing with arms akimbo), within whose body the worlds are stacked.

## Iconography
The cosmos is drawn as a human figure: the narrow lower legs and feet are the hells (*adhololoka*); the slim waist is the middle world (*madhyaloka*) of humans, marked by the central continents; the broad chest and head are the heavens (*urdhvaloka*), crowned by the *siddhashila*, the crescent abode of the liberated souls at the very top of the skull.

## Significance
Jain cosmology rejects a creator and instead measures an eternal, uncreated universe of fixed proportions. Casting it as a [[motif:cosmic man|cosmic man]] makes the [[motif:macrocosm|macrocosm]] legible at a glance and turns cosmography into an aid to meditation on rebirth and release. The figure is a teaching diagram: to ascend the body is to climb out of *saṃsāra* toward the liberated soul at the crown.

## For artists & game designers
A vertical world literally shaped like a person — hell at the feet, paradise in the skull — is a ready-made tower map where the level layout is also a character.

citations.text: Collette Caillat & Ravi Kumar, *The Jain Cosmology* (1981).

entries.title: Adhai-dvipa Paṭa (Two-and-a-Half Continents of the Middle World)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Jain, India)
entries.summary:
An *Adhai-dvipa paṭa*, a Jain cloth cosmogram mapping the *adhai dvipa* — the 'two and a half continents' that make up the only inhabitable region of the middle world.

## Iconography
The map is rigorously concentric: at the centre sits the rose-form continent of *Jambudvipa* with Mount Meru at its hub, ringed by alternating annular oceans and continents (Lavana ocean, Dhatakikhanda, Kaloda ocean, the inner half of Pushkaravara). A circular mountain wall closes off the inhabited zone, beyond which humans cannot be born.

## Significance
This is the cartography of *manushyaloka*, the human world, a tiny disc within the vast Jain universe. The geometric severity is doctrinal: distances and proportions are scriptural facts, and the diagram functions as both devotional object and mnemonic of the cosmos's measured order. Such *paṭa* were hung for instruction and contemplation.

## For artists & game designers
Concentric ring-oceans and ring-continents make an elegant radial world map — distance from the centre is a clean progression axis.

citations.text: Phyllis Granoff (ed.), *Victorious Ones: Jain Images of Perfection* (2009).

entries.title: Jain Cosmological Map of the Universe — Jambudvipa
entries.creator: Anonymous (Jain, India)
entries.summary:
A large, finely painted Jain map of *Jambudvipa*, the central 'rose-apple continent' at the heart of the cosmos, with the world-mountain Meru at its hub.

## Iconography
Jambudvipa is rendered as a great disc divided by mountain ranges and rivers into the realms of the middle world, with Mount Meru — the cosmic axis — at the exact centre, encircled by rings of ocean and concentric continents. The composition is symmetrical to the point of mandala-like regularity, every river and range placed to scripture.

## Significance
For Jains the universe is uncreated and exactly measured, and Jambudvipa is the navel of that order. Such maps are at once cosmography and meditation aids: tracing the rings is a contemplative descent from the human realm outward into the structure of [[motif:cosmography|cosmography]] itself. The world-mountain motif is shared across South and Inner Asia, a common axis joining Jain, Hindu, and Buddher maps.

## For artists & game designers
The highest-resolution map in this cabinet: a mandala-grade radial continent you can zoom into for districts, rivers, and ranges as discrete regions.

citations.text: Collette Caillat & Ravi Kumar, *The Jain Cosmology* (1981).

entries.title: Siddhachakra (Jain Yantra of the Perfected Ones)
entries.creator: Vinayavijaya and Yaśovijaya (attrib.)
entries.summary:
A *Siddhachakra* (or *Navpad*), the most important Jain devotional yantra — a nine-fold lotus diagram of the supreme beings and qualities of the path.

## Iconography
At the centre is the *Arihanta* (enlightened teacher), surrounded on the petals of a lotus by the other four of the Five Supreme Beings — *Siddha*, *Acharya*, *Upadhyaya*, *Sadhu* — interleaved with the four qualities of right faith, knowledge, conduct, and austerity. Sacred syllables and script fill the field, making the diagram a map of perfection rather than a picture of place.

## Significance
Where the *paṭa* map outer space, the [[motif:siddhachakra|siddhachakra]] maps the spiritual cosmos — the hierarchy of liberated and liberating beings toward whom the soul ascends. It is venerated especially during the Ayambil Oli festival, recited and worshipped as a condensed cosmogram of the Jain goal.

## For artists & game designers
A nine-cell radial 'tech tree' of virtues and ranks — a clean template for a skill or devotion wheel.

citations.text: John E. Cort, *Jains in the World* (2001).

entries.title: Samavasarana (the Divine Preaching-Hall of a Tirthankara)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Jain) — photograph by Atishay
entries.date: modern model (Kundalpur), of an ancient cosmographic form
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 (photo: Atishay). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A three-dimensional model of the *Samavasarana*, the miraculous circular preaching-hall the gods raise around a *Tirthankara* the moment he attains omniscience.

## Iconography
The structure rises in concentric tiers — gold, silver, and jewelled ramparts ringed by moats and gateways — with the enlightened teacher seated at the summit, preaching simultaneously to all directions. Steps and terraces lead the assembled beings (humans, gods, animals) up through the rings toward the centre.

## Significance
The Samavasarana is a cosmogram in built form: a perfectly concentric, axially centred world generated by enlightenment itself. Like Mount Meru and the *adhai-dvipa* map, it expresses the Jain conviction that supreme reality is exactly ordered and ascending. The plan echoes the [[motif:mandala-plan|mandala-plan]] shared with the wider Indic world-mountain tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A tiered concentric arena with gates and moats — a literal boss-arena cosmogram you can ascend ring by ring.

citations.text: Phyllis Granoff (ed.), *Victorious Ones: Jain Images of Perfection* (2009).

entries.title: The Fourteen Ratna of the Chakravartin (Jain Manuscript Folio)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Jain, India)
entries.summary:
A Jain manuscript folio depicting the fourteen *ratna* ('jewels' or treasures) of the *Chakravartin*, the universal world-emperor who rules the whole human continent.

## Iconography
The folio sets out, in tidy registers, the fourteen possessions that define a *Chakravartin* — among them the spinning discus-wheel (*chakra*), the umbrella, the gem, the queen, the elephant, the horse, the general, the householder — each a token of sovereignty over Jambudvipa.

## Significance
The Chakravartin is the political mirror of the cosmographic order: just as the *Tirthankara* perfects the spiritual cosmos, the world-emperor unifies the inhabited continent, his discus rolling unopposed to its mountain walls. The fourteen jewels are a canonical list, and depicting them is itself a way of mapping the extent and structure of the human world. The motif crosses into Buddhist *cakravartin* imagery as well.

## For artists & game designers
A ready relic-set: fourteen named 'jewels of empire' as collectible artefacts that together unlock world-rulership.

citations.text: John E. Cort, *Jains in the World* (2001).

entries.title: Illustration of the Mount Sumeru-Centred Universe
entries.creator: A. D. Prakasa (modern rendering of a traditional schema)
entries.summary:
A clear diagram of the Buddhist *Sumeru* (Meru) universe — the world-mountain at the axis of the cosmos, ringed by oceans, mountain walls, and four island-continents.

## Iconography
Mount Sumeru rises at the centre as a great squared mountain, broader at the top than the base, its sides the colours of four jewels. Concentric ranges and seas surround it, and at the cardinal points float the four continents — the southern *Jambudvipa* being the human world. Sun and moon circle the mountain's flanks; the heavens of the gods tier upward above its peak.

## Significance
This is the master-schema of [[motif:buddhist cosmology|Buddhist cosmology]] inherited from the Abhidharma, and the template behind the Tibetan, Nepali, Burmese, and Thai cosmic maps. The vertical stack — hells below, human continents at the waterline, god-realms above — encodes the whole ladder of rebirth on a single mountain. The same axis underlies the mandala offering, in which the cosmos is given to the Buddhas.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical 'world-mountain': a single central megastructure with tiered god-realms above and hells below — the cleanest possible cosmology in one silhouette.

citations.text: Akira Sadakata, *Buddhist Cosmology: Philosophy and Origins* (1997).

entries.title: Cosmological Mandala with Mount Meru
entries.creator: Anonymous (Yuan-dynasty China / Tibetan Buddhist tradition)
entries.repository: The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
entries.summary:
A celebrated silk *Cosmological Mandala with Mount Meru*, a Yuan-dynasty painting that diagrams the Buddhist universe as a geometric mandala.

## Iconography
At the centre stands Mount Meru, drawn as a stepped hourglass column. Around it concentric squares and circles map the ring-mountains and ring-oceans, with the four island-continents at the cardinal points and the sun and moon as discs to either side. The palette is restrained — gold, red, and ink — and the whole is laid out with diagrammatic precision rather than landscape illusion.

## Significance
The painting marries the [[motif:cosmography|cosmography]] of Mount Meru with the mandala's square-in-circle geometry, turning the universe-map into an object of contemplation and ritual offering. Its survival in the Met makes it one of the most reproduced Asian cosmograms in the West, a touchstone for how Buddhist cosmology is visualised.

## For artists & game designers
A museum-grade square-in-circle world plan — perfect reference for a tarot-flat, diagrammatic map screen.

citations.text: Akira Sadakata, *Buddhist Cosmology: Philosophy and Origins* (1997).

entries.title: Traibhumi (Three Worlds) — Thai Cosmological Imagery
entries.creator: Anonymous (Thai, Buddhist)
entries.date: object photographed at the Bangkok National Museum (traditional schema, 18th–19th c. type)
entries.summary:
A Thai depiction of the *Traibhumi* ('Three Worlds') cosmology, the Theravāda map of the realms of desire, form, and formlessness that structures Siamese Buddhist cosmography.

## Iconography
The Three Worlds are shown as a tiered universe rising around Mount Meru: the lower world of desire with its hells, animals, hungry ghosts, humans and lower gods; the middle world of pure form inhabited by the Brahma gods; and the topmost formless realms. Registers of figures and architecture mark each station of rebirth.

## Significance
The *Traibhumikatha* ('Sermon on the Three Worlds'), traditionally ascribed to King Lithai of Sukhothai (14th c.), is the founding text of Thai [[motif:buddhist cosmology|Buddhist cosmology]], and its imagery furnished temple murals and folding-book manuscripts for centuries. The vertical hierarchy of realms is simultaneously a map of the cosmos and a chart of karma — where a being is reborn depends on its deeds.

## For artists & game designers
A stacked 'three worlds' vertical map (desire / form / formless) is a tidy progression of zones from dense matter to pure mind.

citations.text: Frank E. Reynolds & Mani B. Reynolds (trans.), *Three Worlds According to King Ruang* (1982).

entries.title: Burmese Cosmology Manuscript — the Himavanta Forest
entries.creator: Anonymous (Burmese, Buddhist)
entries.summary:
A panel from a Burmese cosmology manuscript showing the *Himavanta* (Himalayan) forest, the legendary highland where Buddhas and ascetics meditate within the larger Mount Meru universe.

## Iconography
The folding-book panel paints a dense forested landscape of lakes, trees, and mythical animals — the great Anotatta lake from which the cosmic rivers flow, the herds and birds of the enchanted wood — set in the foothills of the world-mountain where holy beings retreat from the world of desire.

## Significance
Burmese *kammavaca* and cosmology *parabaik* translated the abstract Meru schema into richly painted scenes, and the Himavanta forest is a favourite station: a paradisal wilderness midway up the cosmic ladder. Such manuscripts were teaching and merit-making objects, mapping the [[motif:cosmography|cosmography]] of rebirth in vivid, narrative form.

## For artists & game designers
A paradisal mythic-forest biome — sacred lakes, exotic beasts — as a contemplative zone perched between the human world and the heavens.

citations.text: Patricia Herbert, *The Life of the Buddha* (1993).

entries.title: Medicinal Flora and Fauna (Blue Beryl Medical Painting)
entries.creator: Workshop of Sangye Gyatso (Desi Sangye Gyamtso)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: after the Lhasa Blue Beryl set]
entries.summary:
A leaf from the great Tibetan medical-painting set commissioned under the regent *Desi Sangye Gyatso* to illustrate the *Blue Beryl* commentary on the *Four Tantras*, here cataloguing the medicinal flora and fauna of the Medicine Buddha's healing realm.

## Iconography
The painting arrays plants, minerals, and animals in tidy diagrammatic rows, each a source of remedy gathered around the celestial city of *Bhaiṣajyaguru*, the Medicine Buddha, whose paradise of healing herbs frames the *materia medica*. The format is encyclopaedic — picture as chart — rather than narrative.

## Significance
Tibetan medicine fused cosmology, embryology, and pharmacology into a single visual system; the *Blue Beryl* set (and the related anatomical and embryological thangkas) maps the body and its cures as part of the same ordered cosmos governed by the [[motif:vajrayana|Vajrayana]] Medicine Buddha. These paintings are among the most systematic 'science illustrations' of pre-modern Asia.

## For artists & game designers
An in-world herbarium / bestiary screen — alchemical ingredients laid out as a gridded, paintable catalogue.

citations.text: Yuri Parfionovitch, Fernand Meyer & Gyurme Dorje, *Tibetan Medical Paintings* (1992).

entries.title: The Golden Cosmic Egg (Hiranyagarbha) by Manaku
entries.creator: Manaku of Guler
entries.summary:
A Pahari painting by the master *Manaku of Guler* of the *Hiraṇyagarbha*, the golden cosmic egg or 'golden womb' from which the universe is born.

## Iconography
A luminous golden egg floats on the dark primordial waters, often shown with the creator (Brahma, or Vishnu reclining) within or beside it, the unmanifest cosmos held in a single radiant ovoid before it splits to form heaven and earth.

## Significance
The *Brahmāṇḍa* — literally 'egg of Brahma' — is one of the oldest Indic cosmogonic images, reaching back to the *Ṛgveda*'s hymn of the [[motif:cosmic egg|golden germ]]. Manaku's series for the *Bhagavata Purana* and related texts rendered these metaphysical first-moments with extraordinary economy: the whole of creation as one floating egg. The motif parallels cosmic-egg myths across Eurasia.

## For artists & game designers
A single glowing ovoid on a void of water — a near-perfect 'genesis' splash image or world-seed icon.

entries.title: Vishnu Vishvarupa (the Universal Form)
entries.creator: Anonymous (North Indian)
entries.summary:
A painting of the *Vishvarupa*, the 'Universal Form' of Vishnu (revealed by Krishna to Arjuna in the *Bhagavad Gita*), in which the entire cosmos is seen contained within a single divine body.

## Iconography
The god is shown with innumerable heads, arms, and eyes, his body filled with tier upon tier of worlds, gods, sages, and creatures — the whole of existence packed into the deity's frame. Mouths blaze with fire that devours the warriors of time; the figure is at once awe-inspiring and terrifying, exactly as Arjuna beholds it.

## Significance
The [[motif:vishvarupa|Vishvarupa]] is the supreme Hindu image of the cosmos-as-body: where the Jain Loka Purusha is the universe shaped *like* a man, the Vishvarupa is the universe *as* God, theophany and cosmogram together. The eleventh chapter of the Gita, in which it appears, is among the most quoted passages of world religious literature.

## For artists & game designers
A single colossal figure whose body contains a teeming world of smaller scenes — superb reference for a 'god-of-everything' boss or loading illustration.

citations.text: Diana L. Eck, *Darśan: Seeing the Divine Image in India* (1998).

entries.title: Samudra Manthan (Churning of the Ocean of Milk)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Indian)
entries.summary:
A painting of the *Samudra Manthan*, the churning of the cosmic ocean of milk by which the gods and demons together wrest the elixir of immortality from the deep.

## Iconography
Mount Mandara is used as a churning-staff, set on the back of Vishnu's tortoise avatar *Kurma*, with the great serpent *Vasuki* wound round it as a rope. The *devas* pull at one end and the *asuras* at the other, and from the foaming ocean rise the treasures of the world — the goddess Lakshmi, the moon, the wish-cow, the physician Dhanvantari with the pot of *amrita*.

## Significance
The churning is a cosmogonic myth of the ordering of the world out of primal flux, a tug-of-war between opposing powers that yields both poison and nectar. It is one of the most depicted scenes in Hindu art (and on the walls of Angkor), a [[motif:cosmogony|cosmogony]] enacted as cooperative violence around the world-axis of Mount Mandara.

## For artists & game designers
A dynamic 'two factions turning the world-engine' set-piece — a cooperative-yet-competitive mechanic with rare loot rising from the churn.

citations.text: Wendy Doniger, *The Hindus: An Alternative History* (2009).

entries.title: Samudra Manthan with Kurma Supporting the Cosmos (1801 Sketch)
entries.creator: Anonymous (South Indian draughtsman)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: record of the Meenakshi temple, Madurai]
entries.summary:
An 1801 line sketch recording a Madurai temple depiction of *Kurma*, Vishnu's tortoise avatar, bearing Mount Mandara on his back as the axis of the churning cosmos.

## Iconography
The tortoise squats at the base supporting the world-mountain used as a churning-pole, the serpent Vasuki coiled around it. Drawn as a clean documentary outline rather than a coloured painting, it isolates the structural idea: the cosmos resting on the back of the tortoise-god.

## Significance
The [[motif:kurma avatar|Kurma]] episode supplies one of the most widespread of all cosmic-support images — the world borne on a tortoise — and its early-colonial line-record here shows how such temple iconography was catalogued. As a draughtsman's outline it complements the painted *Samudramanthan*, foregrounding the architecture of the myth over its colour.

## For artists & game designers
A crisp line-art reference for the 'world on a tortoise' trope — useful as an austere diagram or tattoo-style emblem.

citations.text: Wendy Doniger, *The Hindus: An Alternative History* (2009).

entries.title: Uma-Maheshvara on Mount Kailash (Pahari-Punjab Mural)
entries.creator: Kangra/Jalandhar muralists (one named Sarfuddin)
entries.summary:
A Punjab-hills mural of *Uma-Maheshvara* — Shiva and Parvati enthroned together on Mount Kailash, the sacred world-axis at the centre of the Hindu and Tibetan cosmos.

## Iconography
Shiva (Maheshvara) sits with his consort Uma (Parvati) upon the snow-peak of Kailash, attended by his bull Nandi and the family of gods. The mountain is rendered as the serene seat of the divine couple, paradise atop the world.

## Significance
Mount Kailash is the most potent of Asia's holy mountains, revered as the home of Shiva by Hindus, as Meru's earthly counterpart, and as a sacred pilgrimage peak by Buddhists, Jains, and Bön alike. The [[motif:world-axis|world-axis]] here is not a diagram but a dwelling: the cosmic mountain humanised as a household of gods. Such Pahari-tradition murals carried this imagery into village shrines.

## For artists & game designers
The 'summit throne of the gods' — a snow-peak sanctuary as the literal top of the world-map, reward for the longest climb.

citations.text: Diana L. Eck, *India: A Sacred Geography* (2012).

entries.title: Nataraja (Shiva as Lord of the Cosmic Dance)
entries.creator: Anonymous (South India), photo by Akhilan
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (photo: Akhilan). Object public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An 18th-century South Indian bronze of *Nataraja*, Shiva as Lord of the Dance, whose *ananda tandava* enacts the creation, maintenance, and dissolution of the entire cosmos.

## Iconography
Four-armed Shiva dances within a flaming aureole (*prabhamandala*): one hand holds the drum (*damaru*) that beats out creation, another the fire (*agni*) of destruction; a third gestures 'fear not' (*abhaya*), the fourth points to the raised foot of release. He treads upon the dwarf *Apasmara*, the demon of ignorance, his hair flying out with the river Ganga and a crescent moon.

## Significance
The [[motif:nataraja|Nataraja]] is a cosmogram in bronze: every attribute encodes a cosmic function, the ring of fire is the wheel of *saṃsāra* and the bounds of the universe, and the dance is time itself. Ananda Coomaraswamy's essay 'The Dance of Shiva' made the image a touchstone of how art can compress a whole cosmology into a single pose.

## For artists & game designers
A figure whose every limb is a verb (create / preserve / destroy / release) inside a literal ring of fire — an iconic, readable 'cosmic boss' silhouette.

citations.text: Ananda K. Coomaraswamy, *The Dance of Śiva* (1918).
